chapters.title: There is a technique to bringing high-tech disruptive products into the market; fail to understand it at your own peril
chapters.text:
In the last two decades, thousands of tech startups have arisen all with the hope of disrupting the market and making headway.

But it’s sad that many of these startups do not pass the one year test. Some experience some measure of growth for a year or two, only to start nosediving and being overtaken by competitors.

What has been responsible for this? How can you accurately predict if a startup would thrive or not?

You can’t be entirely correct if you decide quality would be the only metric for measuring business success. A good chunk of all the startups that have failed had excellent products. If you however begin to understudy their marketing tactics, then a new light would open up to you.

It’s not just enough to bring disruptive products to the market, you have to understand consumer behavior and know how to play the markets. There are two categories of innovation: discontinuous or disruptive innovation and continuous or sustaining innovation.

Continuous innovations do not require behavioral changes on the part of the consumers, because those innovations are usually a build up on existing technologies. Example of continuous innovation is when Toyota promises you a faster sports car that doesn’t consume much gasoline.

When you get the car, you wouldn’t have much to learn because you’ve been driving similar vehicles all your life.

How about Tesla introducing a self-driving electric car? Sure enough, such vehicles are better for the environment and may even increase your productivity, but you would need to learn how to control them. Your behavior would need to change when you swap to using electric cars. That’s what disruptive products do: they change consumer behavior, and consumers don’t all adapt at the same speed, so there’s a problem.

Right there is the main reason startups find it difficult to introduce their disruptive products to the market. In trying to market their products, they discover that there are chasms across their row of potential customers.

Once you understand the different customer categories when it comes to high tech products, you will begin to gain insight into how you can market to them effectively.

When it comes to being tech-savvy, not all customers are created equal.

The five groups of potential customers in the market are:
• Innovators
• Early adopters
• Early majority
• Late majority
• Laggards

The rest of this summary is dedicated to exploring these groups and understanding how to meet their unique marketing needs.

chapters.title: The technology adoption life cycle and how it should shape your marketing
chapters.text:
In the last chapter, we had an introduction into the world of high-tech products and how there’s a disparity in the way actual and potential customers react to new products coming into the market.

The most important department in any business is sales and marketing. It’s strange that these two departments — some companies actually merge them into one — are often the most overlooked. To prove this point, when a company enters a financial crisis and wants to cut off some expenses, what department do they go to first? That’s right, marketing!

It’s pathetic but it happens more often than not. The faster business leaders agree that sales and marketing are the lifeblood of every business, the better it would be for them. And this couldn’t be more true for high-tech startups.

Let’s consider the five groups of customers you would encounter in the market.

Chasm crossing is not the end, but rather the beginning, of mainstream market development. ~ Geoffrey A. Moore

The innovators

This is the first group of people to test out your tech innovation. They are the techies of the industry. A very minute group of people who keep their eyes and ears open for creative innovations coming into the industry. You don’t need to market to these ones. So long as your product is good, innovative and capable of making people curious, they are just a website content or an ad away.

Don’t try to sell to innovators, use them to test the waters so you know how your product is likely to perform in the market.

Most times, innovators would want to test your product for free or at a very small price. It’s wise to let them do this. Your point with this market is just to test the viability of your new product. Don’t forget to ask for their critique. Tech enthusiasts are a great asset when you’re seeking to test and improve your new tech product.

chapters.title: Most of your marketing resources should be directed at the early adopters, the pragmatists and the late majority
chapters.text:
The early adopters

These people are the visionaries. They are business leaders with grand business objectives and are open to any new tech product that can help them achieve their goals. They are the real risk takers. Their budgets run into millions and they don’t mind investing huge sums into your product, provided there’s a chance to hit their business goals. A good example of a business visionary is Henry Ford, when he decided to mass produce cars and make them so affordable no household in America would have an excuse for not buying one.

The early majority or pragmatists

Unlike the visionaries, this category of people are not risk takers… well, they take risks when absolutely necessary, but for the most part, risk is a negative word in their vocabulary.

Pragmatists only go for a product when they have seen that other people have fared well using it. They don’t want to be the ones testing your bugs for you. But there’s an upside to this. This category makes up one-third of the entire technology adoption cycle. They are where the profit lies. If you’re able to get them to use your product, then be rest assured of explosive business growth.

When pragmatists like your product, they make it an industry standard and compel everyone in their organization to use it.

To get the attention of pragmatists, you have to take your products where they are. This means industry-specific workshops, trade fairs and conferences. Your name also needs to be mentioned in magazines and newsletters they trust. Bottom line, be on their face!

The late majority

They share the same traits as the early majority, one major additional one: whereas the early majority are comfortable using a technology product once they decide to go for it, people in the late majority don’t enjoy the behavioral changes that come with adopting new tech. This class is older, less educated and less affluent so they don’t exactly see the worth of adopting new tech. It usually takes a lot of price cutting to get them to adopt a new product.

The laggards

These people don’t like anything tech. They are usually the grandparents, the kids who don’t need your product or the less exposed people in third world nations. They adopt tech only when they have no option.

chapters.title: You will encounter a chasm in the marketplace as you try expanding
chapters.text:
There’s a chasm in the technology adoption life cycle. It’s found between pragmatists and the early majority.

As you expand your market base from innovators to early adopters, you will begin to find it a bit difficult to cross over to the mainstream market — the early majority. What happens here is that in this stage, the early majority, a population of mostly young people ready to try new things out, are still testing to see if your product is good enough to be adopted. And while that is happening, your competitors have been alarmed and would instinctively strengthen their sales force in an attempt to ensure they don’t lose market share.

If that isn’t enough, you’re running low on budget already. Much of the funds gained from venture capitalists have been spent and you’re now under pressure to prove yourself to your investors. There’s a real struggle here.

Most startups get stuck in this chasm for months or even a few years. This is also the place where the road ends for some of these startups.

There’s however a system you can use to maneuver your way through the chasm. It’s called niche marketing. When companies get to this stage of their growth, they tend to take the sales approach, in hopes of covering as much ground as possible… at least to get investors off their necks.

The problem is, the sales approach doesn’t work because it’s focused on getting customers across different market segments: lawyers, doctors, engineers, students, basically anyone that looks like a good prospect. You can afford to do this if you’re a large company or if you have zero competition. But if none of those fit your description, then you’re better off taking the niche marketing approach.

You can also call it the big fish in a small pond concept.

Winning at marketing more often than not means being the biggest fish in the pond. If we are very small, then we must search out a very small pond, a target market segment that fits our size. ~ Geoffrey A. Moore

The whole idea behind it is to find a small segment of the market you can serve, dominate and keep your competitors off of. Just one segment, don’t do two or three. Concentrate your efforts on this one segment and win them over.

Startups should consider niche marketing because it’s cost effective and it helps you easily address your customers’ pain points.

Did you know? A major advantage of niche marketing is that you get more word of mouth advertising, which gives you a big shot at conquering the market.

chapters.title: Use whole products as a niche market domination strategy
chapters.text:
If you have a major competitor in the market, then you have to do more than just find a niche market — you have to give the market a strong reason to choose you and not the competitor.

Ideally, when choosing a niche market, you have to go for the market that’s easiest to dominate. In other words, go for markets that have a compelling reason to buy your product. Your chances are higher that way. After establishing this, your next task is to provide the market with “whole products.”

Positioning is the single largest influence on the buying decision. ~ Geoffrey A. Moore

Here’s the thing. When people purchase a high-tech product, they almost always require an additional product or service to effectively use the purchased product.

For example, when you buy an iPad, you’ll need Internet connection — Wi-Fi or cellular — to use it effectively. But not just that, you’d also need augmented products like email, calendar, search engine, app store, etc.

There’s also the chance for potential products like monetized apps on the app store.

If you dedicate the time to brainstorm the supplementary products your target customers need, you would come up with quite a number and when you provide them with these at little to no costs, you would certainly become the go-to supplier of such whole products, thereby achieving your market domination aim.

To brainstorm supplementary products, you need to:
• Study the market to identify their pain, pleasure and desires
• Ask your workers, especially the ones whose jobs require direct contact with your customers

You won’t always be the manufacturer of the supplementary products your market needs. If this happens to you, then make allies with companies that manufacture such supplementary products.

chapters.title: Your price doesn’t have to be constant, the market you’re selling to determines the price you set
chapters.text:
To please each market in the tech adoption cycle, you have to tailor not just your product, but your customer service and price, to their unique needs.

When delivering to pragmatists, for example, you can afford to increase your price and maximize profit because this group doesn’t care so much about price as they do about how your product would help them achieve their business objectives. The method of delivery you chose for this group should be a little more formal because they are mostly business executives and would appreciate your customer service. They also need to know that you’re accessible just in case they need to further work with you.

If you want to capture the majority of your target market, then you have to make your innovative product so good people can’t ignore it, then make it affordable.

After the pragmatists is the early majority or the mainstream market. They are where the real money lies — and it’s not just because of their numbers, it’s also because they are a group of (mostly) young people who know how to leverage on tech and get ahead in their careers. However, you need to reduce your price to somewhere just above the cost price, if you want to win their hearts. But you usually don’t have to worry much about investing in delivery options as you can use any industry delivery method at your disposal. They really don’t care about it as much as the pragmatists. But of course, there’s nothing wrong in doing something that would make you stand out.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Many tech startups fail because they don’t understand how the tech adoption life cycle works. If you’re bringing any disruptive product to the market, then it’s profitable to take the time and really understand how the adoption life cycle works. Or better still, hire a tech marketing consultant.

One mistake most startups make is that they think the early adopters are no different from the early majority, thus they treat them the same way and wonder why their products suddenly fail in the market. In order to avoid this costly mistake, take the time to study the difference between these two groups and device means to serve them uniquely.

The early adopters are those folks with grand business plans and deep pockets, people who don’t mind spending money on your product, provided it can help them achieve their business objective. They are all about the objective.

The early majority on the other hand don’t have deep pockets so they are careful to only buy new products when they see good testimonials from others. Your best bet therefore is to create products that solve real business problems. Once you pass that test, you can be rest assured that you would sell well.

Try this

Nearly every disruptive product would encounter a chasm in the market. The earlier you prepare for this, the better for your organization. Begin right now to find a niche market that you would focus on and establish your dominion. Do this effectively, and you will cross the chasm in no time.
